other_observers: Joe Roller, Andy Boyce, Mark Peterson, Connie Kogler; Both Mark Peterson and I independently found the bird while scanning from different parts of the reservoir, and the identification was reached by the two of us with Joe Roller and Andy Boyce.
specific_location: Seen from the dam at the north end of Prewitt Reservoir
description: the bird was a tad smaller than the other Long-tailed Jaeger that was present, when seen close together, and significantly smaller than a Ring-billed Gull. The wings were long and narrow, including at the base. The bird overall had a sleek, thin look, even compared to the other Long-tailed present. The back was a cold brownish color with a grayish tinge, not especially dark. The head had a "hood" of near the same color, but a bit warmer brown. The nape was paler brown than the hood, very hard to see on the bird unless is banked at close distance. The belly was white with a very few scattered brown marks, especially along the flanks. The undertail coverts were stongly barred with dark brown on white, as were the uppertail coverts. The tail was the same color brown, and the central retrices were blunt-tipped, but barely stuck out and were very hard to see. The tail overall looked fairly long. The underwings were very boldly checked with dark brown/black and white. The base of the primaries below had a bold white flash, but there was no "double flash" created with the coverts. The upperside of the primaries at most times did not appear to have any white on them, but the one time the bird banked when it was close I saw a very small amount of white on the outermost primaries, and I feel there were not more than two white primary shafts. Other than noticing the bill looked small I did not notice any details here.Behaviors: The bird would circuit the reservoir, flying with fairly shallow wing beats at a fairly constant height. It rarely banked or dove to the water. Towards the end of the observation it was observed close to the other Long-tailed Jaeger present, and was observed to be smaller and even sleeker than that bird. This bird was never observed to harrass any bird, or to feed. It was only seen on the water briefly, once. This was used as supporting evidence in identification of this bird.Call: not heardPlumage: light phase juvenile
